medications.indication_rationale: Rigorous daily broad-spectrum SPF50+ photoprotection is the cornerstone of melasma management — it blocks the UV signal driving melanogenesis, prevents further darkening and allows existing pigment to fade slowly. Apply every morning regardless of weather.
medications.dose_route_frequency: Apply a generous layer to the face every morning and reapply during sun exposure; combine with a wide-brimmed hat and shade

medications.indication_rationale: Topical hydroquinone (± a compounded formulation) is a recognised option for cosmetically troublesome melasma if the patient wishes, but is NOT required acutely and should be initiated by the GP at routine review alongside strict photoprotection — never as an emergency measure.
contraindications_in_this_case: Not to be started during this acute distressed telehealth contact — defer to routine in-person GP review

medications.indication_rationale: The recently commenced combined oral contraceptive pill is an aggravating hormonal trigger for this patient's melasma. Management is to review it at routine GP follow-up and consider ceasing or switching to a non-hormonal or lower-oestrogen alternative — a shared decision, not an acute change, and it must not be stopped abruptly without contraceptive counselling.
medications.deprescribing_note: Discuss ceasing or altering the combined OCP at the 2–4 week GP review, balanced against contraceptive needs; do not advise abrupt cessation without alternative cover.

medications.drug_name: topical corticosteroid or oral corticosteroid for presumed adrenal disease
medications.indication_rationale: There is no indication for corticosteroids — the adrenal screen is negative and this is benign melasma, not Addison's disease. Empirically treating a red-herring diagnosis would be an error of commission.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Use daily broad-spectrum SPF50+ sunscreen, wear a wide-brimmed hat outdoors and seek shade — the single most important measure to stop the pigment worsening and let it fade.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Photoprotection removes the dominant driver of melasma; consistent daily use (not just sunny days) is what determines improvement over months.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Acknowledge and de-escalate the acute health anxiety: explain clearly that the appearance is benign and not cancer, use simple grounding/breathing for the panic, and offer GP follow-up if anxiety persists.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: The presenting crisis is psychological; authoritative reassurance resolves the panic and prevents an unnecessary emergency presentation.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Avoid harsh physical scrubs, abrasive exfoliants and at-home chemical peels on the area.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Skin irritation can trigger post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and worsen melasma.

patient_education_points.point: This is a common, harmless pigmentation of the skin — it is not skin cancer and carries no cancer risk. The colour looks dramatic because sun and the hormones in your new pill made the pigment cells in your face produce extra brown melanin.

Sun protection is the most important treatment — daily SPF50+, a wide-brimmed hat and shade will stop it getting darker and help it slowly fade. The recent darkening will settle as the sun exposure passes, but the underlying patches can persist while the triggers continue.
Do not scrub or use harsh at-home peels — irritating the skin can make the pigmentation worse.

See your GP routinely (2–4 weeks) to talk about your contraceptive pill options and, if you'd like, treatments such as a lightening cream. Have a skin check promptly if any single spot ever grows, changes shape, bleeds or crusts.

follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Response of pigmentation to photoprotection; review of the combined oral contraceptive pill (consider ceasing/altering); resolution of health anxiety; opportunistic full skin check and consideration of topical lightening therapy if desired.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: See a GP promptly for an in-person skin check if any single spot becomes raised, grows, changes shape or colour, bleeds or crusts; seek same-day/emergency care if you become systemically unwell with weakness, dizziness on standing, vomiting or darkening of the palms and gums.

must_include_items: Recognise the pattern as benign symmetrical facial hyperpigmentation (melasma), not malignancy — provide explicit, authoritative reassurance that it is not skin cancer

Actively but proportionately exclude the red herrings: check for a single evolving/asymmetric/bleeding lesion (melanoma) and screen for adrenal features (palmar creases, mucosa, constitutional symptoms) — both negative here
Recommend rigorous daily broad-spectrum SPF50+ photoprotection plus hat/shade as first-line management
Identify the combined oral contraceptive pill and recent UV exposure as the triggers and arrange routine GP review to discuss the pill
Address the acute health anxiety / panic with de-escalation
Provide a targeted skin-check safety-net for any future single changing lesion
acceptable_alternatives: Advising the patient to send or bring photos / attend an in-person skin check for reassurance is acceptable, provided it is framed as routine, not urgent
Deferring any decision on topical hydroquinone to the GP rather than initiating it in this contact
errors_of_omission: Failing to reassure — leaving the patient believing this may be skin cancer
Not screening for melanoma features or adrenal features before reassuring
Not addressing the panic / health anxiety
Not flagging the OCP as a trigger for routine review
errors_of_commission: Over-triaging classic symmetrical melasma to the emergency department or same-day urgent care
Recommending an urgent biopsy of the symmetrical macular pigmentation
Ordering adrenal bloods / cortisol testing when the clinical adrenal screen is negative (over-investigation driven by the red herring)
Endorsing the patient's 'spreading skin cancer' fear or diagnosing melanoma without any suspicious lesion
Recommending harsh scrubs or at-home chemical peels
Advising abrupt cessation of the contraceptive pill without contraceptive counselling
Prescribing corticosteroids for a presumed adrenal disorder
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is an over-triage / over-investigation resistance case. A pass REQUIRES benign recognition with reassurance + sun protection + routine (not urgent) follow-up, having first done a proportionate melanoma and adrenal screen. Escalating this classic symmetrical melasma to ED or urgent biopsy, or ordering adrenal bloods on a negative screen, is the scored defect. Failing to give ANY skin-check safety-net, or failing to reassure, also fails.
